summary: Gruber draws a sharp distinction between copying ideas and copying implementations, coining the term 'design plagiarism' to describe the latter. Responding to readers who cited an Ars Technica piece about iOS borrowing features from Android, he argues that implementing similar features is normal competition, not copying. The key test is whether the implementations are visually distinguishable — iOS and Android clearly are, while Huawei's Portrait mode was a direct rip-off of Apple's. He likens it to writing: covering the same topic is fair game, but lifting someone's words is plagiarism. In a footnote, he revisits Apple's 1990s look-and-feel lawsuit against Microsoft, arguing it was misguided because Windows wasn't a copy of the Mac — Apple was trying to own the basic concept of a GUI.
key_points: There is a fundamental difference between copying an idea (implementing a similar feature) and copying an implementation (replicating the specific design)
Competition naturally involves companies following each other's feature innovations, and that's healthy, not plagiarism
The test for design plagiarism is whether implementations are distinguishable — iOS and Android always are, Huawei's Portrait mode and Apple's were not
Apple's 1990s look-and-feel lawsuit against Microsoft was a mistake because it tried to own the basic concept of a GUI rather than a specific implementation
Good ideas are meant to spread; trying to prevent that is both futile and counterproductive
money_quotes: There's a difference between copying an idea and copying an implementation of that idea.
If you write an article, and then I write my own article about the same topic, that just means you were first. But if I copy your article and just change a few words, that's plagiarism.
This is not copying. Following? Sure. That's how competition works.
Do any of these screenshots leave even one iota of confusion regarding which is iOS and which is Android? No.
Windows was clearly not a rip-off or copy of the Mac. There was no confusion which was which, and the Mac was elegant and Windows — especially pre-Windows 95 — was simply gross.
Apple wasn't trying to prevent Microsoft from copying the Mac — they were trying to prevent Microsoft from using the basic idea of a windows / icons / mouse pointer GUI.
Good ideas are meant to spread.
key_insight: The line between healthy competition and design plagiarism lies not in whether you implement the same idea, but in whether your implementation is distinguishably your own.
